data.title: Amazon Stock Slide Is An Opportunity To Pounce
data.description:
Introduction

Amazon (AMZN) is the last Mag 7 that I have yet to review, and I believe that is the right time to establish coverage based on the recent quarterly earnings that pushed the stock to decline sharply. FY25 was a structural pivot for the company as it moved from the margin-focused year of efficiency toward a capital-intensive, high-stakes expansion into generative AI and global satellite connectivity, and the recent dropoff might just leave an opportunity to buy in.

Current Dynamics

Amazon posted a mixed set of results for its Q4, as GAAP EPS came in at $1.95, a miss of 1 cent, while revenue came in at a massive $213.4B, a beat of $2.17B and a 13.6% Y/Y increase, which is simply incredible. For the full year, revenue reached $716.9B, a 12% increase from the levels seen in 2024. But the composition of this revenue illustrates a deepening shift toward service-based income that encompasses third-party seller services, AWS, advertising, and subscriptions. In total, service sales reached $420.7B for the full year, significantly outpacing net product sales of $296.3B.

The operating income has also drastically increased Y/Y to reach $25B in the fourth quarter, compared to $21.2B, even if it was impacted by several non-recurring special charges. The charges totaled $2.4B and included $1.1 for the resolution of tax disputes and $610MM in asset impairments primarily associated with the physical stores business. On a normalized operating income would have been $27.4B, reflecting an underlying operating margin of 12.8%. Though on the net income side, the miss was largely linked to elevated depreciation expenses and infrastructure outlays associated with the massive increase in capital investment, though it is to be noted that net income was bolstered in late 2025 by a $9.5B non-operating gain from the revaluation of the company’s equity investment in Anthropic.

Diving deeper segment-wise, AWS has remained the primary engine of profitability and a key indicator of enterprise technology health. In Q4, revenue surged 24% Y/Y to reach $35.6B, which represents the fastest growth rate for the segment in thirteen quarters, indicating a definitive reacceleration after the workload optimization phase seen in 2023 and early 2024. This reacceleration is driven by two main factors, the migration of core, non-AI workloads as enterprises return to long-term cloud transformation and the explosive demand for gen AI services. Plus management has highlighted that AWS’s unique top-to-bottom AI stack is gaining significant market share.

A cornerstone of the company’s current AI strategy is the development of in-house silicon to provide superior price-performance for cloud customers. This includes the Graviton CPU and the Trainium AI chips, and the whole segment has surpassed a $10B in annual revenue run rate. Graviton is now used by over 90% of AWS's top 1,000 customers, offering up to 40% better price-performance than standard x86 processors.

Looking at Amazon Bedrock, the managed service for building and scaling generative AI applications, has also achieved a multibillion-dollar annualized run rate, and customer spend on the platform has risen by over 60% on a quarterly basis, which is simply mighty impressive. The backlog provides great visibility into future revenue and can, in part, justify the increase in data center capacity that the company is currently undertaking.

Though the retail segment does not really get the spotlight, it still remains the primary source of cash generation for Amazon, even as its margins are pressured by investment in delivery speed and low-price categories. Looking at N.A., sales grew 10% to $127.1B in Q4, while international segment sales rose 17% to $50.7B. One of the biggest drivers in retail efficiency in 2025 was the regionalization 2.0 strategy, which further optimized the US fulfillment network into eight distinct regional clusters. This shift will allow the company to deliver 8B items same-day or next-day to Prime members, a 40% increase from the 2024 levels.

The advertising services segment continues to be a great performer, acting as a critical high-margin profit driver, offsetting the lower margins of the retail business. Quarterly revenue reached $21.3B, a 22% Y/Y increase, and Amazon added $12B in incremental advertising revenue in 2025 alone. Prime Video Ads has been a strong driver as it reached an average ad-supported audience of 315MM in 2025, up from 200MM at the start of 2024.

So why has the stock dropped so much following the earnings release? Well, the more than 11% drop in after-hours trading was primarily linked to investor concerns over the company’s massive infrastructure spending plans. Management announced a $200B CapEx plan for the year, which is simply eye-popping as it is nearly 60% higher than the $128B spent in 2025 and far exceeds what the street was expecting. Due to the aggressive spending, FCF generation plummeted to $11.2B for the year, down from $38.2B at the end of 2024. Investors reacted nervously to the prospects of the 2026 plan, considering that it will potentially exceed its total operating cash flow.

The cloud competition is also accelerating, as though AWS's numbers were solid, it trails the growth seen at Google Cloud (GOOG) and Microsoft Azure (MSFT), as they both had faster growth rates of 48% and 39% respectively. The company’s operating income guidance for Q1 also came below what analysts were projecting, as it includes an estimated $1B in higher Y/Y costs associated with the accelerating launch schedule of the Leo satellite constellation.

Valuation

Following the current decline in AMZN’s stock, the company is now trading at a P/E Non-GAAP of 28.86, a 5-year low and a significant drop from the peaks of December of 78.2x. The company’s FCF yield has also been drastically reduced due to the Capex plan, reaching 0.3%, the lowest levels seen in positive territory for at least a decade. Looking at the company’s EV/EBITDA, which stands at 15.6x, I expect it to further compress in the next few years as earnings and operating income are set to grow. These heightened valuation metrics are justified in my opinion, considering the incredible moat that Amazon has based on its network effects, cost advantages, switching costs and assets. Truly, the company is assembling the infinity stones equivalent of moats. Amazon emerged as the disruptor in the retail industry, and it now emerges as the leading public cloud service provider via AWS.

Amazon has loads of great growth prospects ahead, but I want to highlight AWS. As I mentioned earlier, it recently reaccelerated to a 24% Y/Y growth rate, its fastest in over three years. The backlog stands at a massive $244B, providing strong multi-year revenue visibility. Plus, the in-house chips have also been a great boon, with demand for Trainium3 already nearly fully committed through mid-2026. Looking at Advertising, it has become the secondary profit engine thanks to the scale of Prime Video Ads, but also Ad-tech efficiency, as the use of Amazon’s demand-side platform is accelerating with clicks increasing 186% Y/Y as AI-powered tools optimize creative campaigns. I’ll also quickly touch on Amazon Leo, which could become a long-term opportunity to provide high-speed, low-latency broadband globally. The network is built for native integration with AWS, targeting remote enterprise sectors such as mining. The newly debuted Leo Ultra antenna supports gigabit-speed downloads, positioning the service to compete directly with existing low-Earth orbit providers.

Risks

The biggest risk right now would be, of course, the aggressive spending trajectory. The CapEx shock will significantly pressure short-term profitability and thus FCF will become increasingly slim in a period where all hyperscalers are fighting ferociously. There is considerable debate about the long-term ROIC that these massive AI investments will be able to garner. If these fail to materialize, Amazon may face massive write-downs on its expensive data center and custom silicon infrastructure.

Amazon’s retail margins are also facing headwinds coming from low-price competition. Amazon has focused on value-oriented retail through Amazon Haul and thus opened itself to competition from the lower-margin product segments that include Temu.

Conclusion

I believe that the drop in post earnings is a strong opportunity to buy shares in a company that has a vast number of moats. While it was dominating retail, Amazon launched AWS and created a whole new industry that it is again dominating. I would not bet on the company, and thus I give it a Buy rating.

data.title: Europe Shows Its Cards
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro and Bert Colijn, Chief Economist, Netherlands

When it comes to using audiovisual promotional materials at least, Europe has clearly caught up with the US. On the European Council's website, today’s so-called informal meeting (these days aka ‘the retreat’) was announced with a fancy video entitled ‘the cards are in our hands’. We still wonder how borrowing elements of the US communication strategy aligns with Europe’s goal of strategic autonomy. But this is a topic for another day.

The subject of today’s meeting was once again centred around the question of how to restore European competitiveness and how to ensure stronger growth in the future. Or, in other words, how to ensure that Europe regains a seat at the table, not by referring to past successes or legacy, but simply because Europe matters globally, both economically and politically.

Fortunately, European leaders did not appear to question the need for action and instead seemed united in recognising the urgency to act. When it comes to how to act, however, views have been and still are less united. In the run-up to today’s meeting, the almost traditional rift between European countries surfaced once again.

While Germany put its money on deregulation and simplification, further integration of the single market, and a more European energy market, France pushed for Made-in-Europe policies to protect domestic industries and more public investments, preferably funded by eurobonds. In the past, these kinds of stand-offs have ended with standstills.

Europe has a plan: One Europe, one market

At least judging from the comments by European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and European Council President Antonia Costa after the meeting, Europe appears to be following the German approach, but without additional fiscal spending.

Europe’s answer to geopolitical shifts, the emergence of China as a systemic rival, and its own lack of strategic autonomy is encapsulated in the initiative “One Europe, one market”, which is expected to be completed by the end of 2027. This plan consists of three main pillars:

  1. Reducing the administrative burden through simplification, an end to the so-called goldplating (i.e., additional national legislation on top of European legislation) and so-called sunset clauses.
  2. Improving the internal market by reviewing merger rules to create global European champions, a capital markets union, and the so-called 28th regime; legislation that allows for the establishment of truly European companies.
  3. Building one energy market with cross-border energy grids.

To underline that at least some European leaders are serious about advancing integration, the European Commission intends to propose more regulations and fewer directives. Regulations must be applied uniformly across all EU member states, whereas directives allow for significant national variation.

In addition, the option of enhanced cooperation was mentioned several times. Enhanced cooperation enables a group of at least nine EU countries to move ahead with further integration. The truth, however, is that this mechanism has never been used in practice for economic policy.

The proof is in the pudding

All of this is promising, even if the practical political hurdles remain significant. Take the aim of ending gold‑plating: it may sound straightforward, but in practice it means transferring national powers to Brussels, which in practice remains a very painful step. And the largest elephant in the room remains the question of how to pay for everything and whether these measures will be enough to really kick-start private investment.

What today’s announcements may amount to is a substantial clean‑up effort coupled with a clear attempt to move closer to a genuine, fully functioning single market, even if achieving the latter will require far more than what leaders discussed today.

Just think of the harmonisation of taxes, pensions, social security, labour laws, to mention a few. Finally, without financial incentives, reviving private investments will remain difficult. So why not start a kind of sovereign wealth fund in Europe, funded by private and public money, and open to retail investors – a fund that then invests in pan-European projects in infrastructure, defence and AI?

Admittedly, we have been following European politics for too long to get excited like teenagers at the first sign of ambition. In fact, Europe has never really suffered from a lack of plans or grand vision; the real obstacle has almost always been weak implementation and national interests taking precedence over European ones.

Whether this time will be any different remains to be seen. The next milestone comes in a month’s time: the European Summit in March, where today’s broad ideas will already need to be translated into concrete action.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Existing Home Sales Sink -8.4% In January
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Existing home sales sank -8.4% in January to their lowest level since September 2024. According to the National Association of Realtors (NAR), existing home sales were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 3.91 million units, falling short of the projected 4.16 million. Existing home sales are down -4.4% compared to one year ago.

"The decrease in sales is disappointing. The below-normal temperatures and above-normal precipitation this January make it harder than usual to assess the underlying driver of the decrease and determine if this month’s numbers are an aberration,” said NAR Chief Economist Dr. Lawrence Yun. “Affordability conditions are improving, with NAR’s Housing Affordability Index showing that housing is the most affordable it’s been since March 2022. This is due to wage gains outpacing home price growth and mortgage rates being lower than a year ago. However, supply has not kept pace and remains quite low.”

"Due to low supply, the median home price reached a new high for the month of January,” Yun added. “Homeowners are in a financially comfortable position as a result. Since January 2020, a typical homeowner would have accumulated $130,500 in housing wealth.”

(Press release)

Background on Existing Home Sales

Existing home sales measures the monthly sales of previously owned single-family homes. It makes up a huge part of the residential real estate market, as 90% of purchased homes are previously owned according to the National Association of Realtors.

More importantly, there exists a strong correlation between purchases of existing homes and consumer spending. An increase in existing home sales can indirectly stimulate economic activity with increased consumer spending on new furnishings and appliances. Alternatively, a sustained drop in existing home sales often foreshadows a downturn in the economy.

Here is a snapshot of the data series, which comes from the National Association of Realtors. The data since January 1999 was previously available in the St. Louis Fed's FRED repository and is now only available for the last twelve months.

Over this time frame, we clearly see the real estate bubble, which peaked in 2005 and then fell dramatically. Sales were volatile for the first year or so following the Great Recession, with monthly sales as low as 3.45 million units to as high as 5.44 million units. We have seen that same volatility following the most recent recession, with sales ranging from 3.85 million units to 6.73 million units.

Existing Home Sales: The Population-Adjusted Reality

Now let's examine the data with a simple population adjustment. The Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates show a 23.6% increase in the US population since the turn of the century. The snapshot below is an overlay of the NAR's annualized estimates with a population-adjusted version.

Existing home sales are 25.2% below the NAR's January 2000 estimate. The population-adjusted version is 38.8% below the turn-of-the-century sales.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

According to Freddie Mac, the average 30-year fixed rate mortgage in January 2026 was 6.10%, the lowest level in over a year. Here is a look back at the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage weekly average, beginning in April 1971.

Existing Home Sales: Median Price

The median price for existing homes fell to its lowest level in a year, coming in at $396,800. This represents a -2.0% decrease from the previous month and a 0.9% increase from one year ago, marking the 31st consecutive month of year-over-year increases.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Allspring Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The Allspring Growth Fund underperformed the Russell 3000 Growth Index during the fourth quarter.
  • Holdings within information technology (IT) and consumer discretionary detracted from returns.
  • Security selection within industrials and health care contributed positively to performance.

Market review - 2025 in review

U.S. equities delivered a third consecutive year of positive returns in 2025, with gains once again concentrated in growth-oriented parts of the market. Stocks in the IT and communication services sectors remained central to index performance as investors continued to reward companies positioned to capture outsized demand from the buildout of artificial intelligence (AI)—particularly those showing business momentum and pricing power.

The year was also a reminder that markets can “climb a wall of worry” even when policy headlines are destabilizing. Early spring delivered the most dramatic test: The White House’s April 2 “Liberation Day” action jolted risk assets, reviving trade-war fears and forcing investors to reconsider inflation and margin assumptions. Over time, the tariff story became more iterative, marked by pauses and modifications that reduced the immediate sense of worst-case outcomes even as it remained a persistent overhang in corporate and consumer sentiment.

Fundamentals ultimately mattered more than headlines. Earnings tied to end markets with strong demand—especially AI—proved resilient, helping offset macroeconomic crosscurrents that ranged from trade policy and politics to immigration debates and gradually cooling labor markets. At the consumer level, conditions looked increasingly uneven: Higher-income households continued to spend, while middle- and lower-income cohorts showed clearer signs of trade-down behavior amid ongoing affordability pressure. Against that backdrop, market breadth improved episodically, but overall performance remained heavily influenced by a concentrated set of large-cap winners—an important context for assessing the outlook as we move forward.

Shifting leadership

AI was a key economic driver in 2025, and leadership broadened within the AI complex. What began as a narrow story dominated by a handful of mega-cap winners increasingly expanded into the infrastructure “picks and shovels” that enable AI deployment—spanning semiconductors, networking, data-center components, and adjacent software ecosystems. That evolution helped reinforce the market’s preference for companies able to convert AI enthusiasm into measurable bookings, backlog, and operating leverage.

At the same time, leadership outside the mega caps was more sensitive to rates and liquidity. As the Federal Reserve (Fed) shifted toward easing in September, risk appetite broadened and participation improved—most visibly in smaller-cap and more cyclically oriented areas. However, the quality of those rallies was mixed as some of the strongest upside phases were led by lower-quality and unprofitable companies, underscoring the difference between liquidity-driven beta surges and fundamentally supported returns. In our view, that distinction became one of the most important “fault lines” in the market and a key lens for evaluating opportunities into 2026.

Year-end cyclical rotation

The fourth quarter added fresh complexity to an already eventful year. Investor attention turned more intensely to the scale, timing, and funding of AI-related capital commitments, prompting renewed debate about whether expectations were running ahead of eventual monetization. This scrutiny contributed to another rotation away from the more crowded AI exposures and toward more cyclical and value-oriented positioning.

Macroeconomic uncertainty also rose during the quarter as the U.S. government shutdown disrupted portions of the data flow and added noise to near-term growth tracking. By late in the quarter, however, markets leaned back toward optimism as the shutdown ended and the Fed’s December messaging reinforced the easing trajectory that began in September, supporting a rebound in broader risk appetite into year-end.

Overall, most major U.S. equity indexes finished the fourth quarter higher while style leadership shifted: Value outperformed growth, reflecting the rotation away from the AI complex into more cyclical positions. The Russell 1000 Value Index returned 3.81% during the quarter, outperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index’s return of 1.12% by nearly 270 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%).

Fund performance and attribution

Top Contributors and Detractors to Quarter-end Fund Performance

CONTRIBUTORS

  • Carpenter Technology Corp.
  • Natera, Inc.
  • BridgeBio Pharma, Inc. (BBIO)
  • argenx SE
  • Penumbra, Inc. (PEN)

DETRACTORS

  • Alphabet Inc.
  • Varonis Systems, Inc. (VRNS)
  • Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN)
  • Oracle Corp.
  • DoorDash, Inc.
The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Discussion of Contributors

Carpenter Technology Corp. (CRS) is prepared for lift-off. The company manufactures premium metal alloys used in highly specialized components across aerospace, medical, and industrial markets. While this diversification provides a degree of demand stability, our investment thesis focuses on the significant replacement cycle in aerospace—a sector that has faced years of underinvestment and production delays. The post-pandemic surge in air travel has led airlines to keep aging fleets in service longer, driving higher maintenance needs. This has boosted demand for Carpenter’s suite of specialty hardware, including bearings, fasteners, and electrical components. Producing these advanced alloys is both complex and capital-intensive, giving Carpenter meaningful pricing power—a fact underscored this quarter as multiple long-term contracts were repriced favorably. With robust product demand and expanding margins, we remain confident in Carpenter’s long-term growth prospects.

Natera, Inc. (NTRA), is redefining precision in diagnostics. A molecular diagnostics company, Natera specializes in three main areas of health care: women’s health, oncology, and organ health. The women’s health division commands nearly half of the noninvasive prenatal testing market, generating wide margins that have fueled innovation across other segments. Leveraging in-house expertise, Natera developed Signatera, a liquid biopsy that detects minimal residual disease and can identify cancer recurrence months before traditional methods. The blood-based test currently holds FDA and Medicare approvals for less than a dozen types of cancer, yet adoption surged during the year due to the minimally invasive approach and early detection capabilities. With additional approvals pending, Natera doubled the number of oncology sales representatives to drive awareness and volumes in 2026. Backed by cutting-edge diagnostics and a vast, underpenetrated market, we believe that Natera is well positioned to transform cancer care and deliver sustained growth for investors.

Immunology and returns are boosting with argenx SE (ARGX). As a commercial-stage biopharmaceutical company, argenx is focused on developing therapies for chronic immunological conditions. Its flagship treatment, VYVGART, targets the harmful antibodies causing disease without shutting down the entire immune system—giving patients strong results with fewer side effects compared with other treatments. Initially, VYVGART was administered intravenously at ambulatory or infusion centers but recently became available in prefilled dosages (PFS). Far from cannibalizing existing IV usage, VYVGART PFS has expanded the company’s addressable market by improving access for patients in communities without local infusion centers. As VYVGART gains traction with new patients, argenx continues to advance its pipeline, targeting additional autoimmune disorders to further broaden its impact.

Discussion of Detractors

AI is spelling opportunity for Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL). In early 2025, investor sentiment declared that Google search was all but dead, replaced by large language models (LLMs) and AI models. Yet, with decades of search data on file, Alphabet had the key components and talent to produce its own top-notch LLM. In September, Google released the “Gemini Drop,” which included new features such as Nano Banana image editing. Daily downloads of Gemini usurped those of ChatGPT, capturing a sliver of the market. Sentiment for Alphabet was further buoyed after the Department of Justice found that Google monopolized search advertising markets yet allowed the company to maintain lucrative partnerships with Chrome or Android. While the quarter was positive for Alphabet, our relative underweight meaningfully detracted from returns. We added to the position during the quarter but have maintained a relative underweight due to the rapidly shifting AI landscape.

Oracle Corp. (ORCL) cools on circular concerns. After years of steady but modest growth from legacy systems, Oracle reinvented itself by transforming its global data center network into Oracle Cloud Infrastructure. In early 2025, the company joined forces with AI heavyweights—including NVIDIA (NVDA) and OpenAI—through the Stargate Project to build next-generation AI-focused data centers across the U.S. Momentum continued as Oracle expanded strategic partnerships with Microsoft (MSFT) and Bloom Energy. This peaked in September when Oracle announced a five-year, $300 billion contract with OpenAI, marking one of the largest AI infrastructure deals ever and the first to be funded primarily through debt. Investor response was initially enthusiastic—the stock spiked over 40%—but the rally reversed as concerns emerged over Oracle’s capital intensity and circular dependency risk. Reflecting these dynamics, we reduced our position due to increased risk and volatility while maintaining conviction that Oracle’s stable core business offers steady cash flow and its AI endeavors provide optionality and growth potential. With improving profitability and clarity on large deals, we believe Oracle can reestablish itself as an AI front-runner in the long term.

DoorDash, Inc. (DASH), hit a speed bump on its growth journey.

The company continues to leverage its scale and advanced logistics platform to expand beyond restaurant delivery into convenience, grocery, and retail categories. While core order volumes accelerated and top-line trends remained strong, the recent quarter was overshadowed by elevated spending on international expansion, technology upgrades, and new verticals. These investments pressured margins and tempered near-term profitability, sparking investor concerns despite healthy demand fundamentals. Longer term, we believe DoorDash’s diversification strategy—coupled with global market penetration and leadership in last-mile logistics—positions it to capture significant share in the evolving delivery ecosystem.

Fund positioning and outlook

Two big narratives

For U.S. equity investors, 2025 will be remembered for two key themes. First, AI continued to seize the spotlight. A super cycle of AI-related investments, which many expected to cool, went into higher gear. The arms race for computing power and infrastructure contributed to prolonged market concentration around leading large-cap technology stocks. The notion that AI would transcend into a broader subset of sectors was largely delayed. Despite the excitement, the year ended with concerns of a potential AI bubble driven by large “circular deals,” rising levels of both public and private debt, and a change in leadership for the market’s favored AI complex.

The second major theme of the year was around leadership from low-quality “non-earners.” “Risk-on” speculation was on the rise, largely driven by fading trade-war fears and an expectation of significant policy stimulus in 2026. The Fed was expected to continue to cut rates and shift in a dovish direction. Additionally, stimulative impacts from the One Big Beautiful Bill were expected to be a tailwind to economic growth.

Quality growth on sale

These factors contributed to the year ending with a divergence in market leadership. Value-oriented and economically sensitive stocks rallied in the fourth quarter, with higher-quality growth stocks generally lagging. The low-quality momentum, which was previously driven by themes like crypto and quantum computing, continued in the fourth quarter but with a different complexion. The darlings of retail traders shifted to commodities, transports, and speculative names in biotechnology.

This speculation resulted in some high-quality growth businesses going on sale. Sitting outside the current zeitgeist, there was a narrowing of the valuation premium awarded to companies that can grow sustainably at faster rates. Based on our experience, this setup is often favorable for future returns. When elite growth franchises de-rate on sentiment factors, compelling entry points can emerge. As the influence of sentiment diminishes over time, the underlying growth of the business reemerges as the dominant driver of returns. With some portfolio holdings going unrewarded for exceptional fundamental results, we are excited at the prospects of strong future returns for these exceptional businesses.

Along the same lines, we remain excited about the emergence of small- and mid-cap equities. While market broadening took on a start/stop pattern, we expect a more sustained leadership shift in 2026. The setup for small- and mid-cap growth stocks remains highly favorable. Relative valuations are historically attractive, earnings growth is expected to close in on large caps, AI-related opportunities are broadening, corporate activity is picking up, and stimulative policy effects are in the system. The stars may be aligning for a period of healthy performance beyond the mega caps dominating U.S. equity benchmarks.

Scarcity of growth

We continue to have high conviction in the scarcity of growth. Unlike the consensus view that economic growth is about to reaccelerate, our view is that growth will remain at modest levels. From the bigger picture, we see structural forces compressing economic output. With only a small number of companies in U.S. equity benchmarks expected to deliver strong future earnings growth, companies with robust fundamentals will remain in short supply. This should benefit quality growth stocks as investors reward companies with sustainable fundamentals.

In closing, our approach is to remain highly disciplined. Rather than chasing short-term speculation, our focus is on identifying the true long-term winners of innovation such as AI, many of which will soon emerge beyond the infrastructure phase. We execute this strategy through stock selection, balanced portfolio construction, and a thoughtful approach to risk management. With economic growth slowing while technological disruption accelerates, focusing on resilient growth businesses on the “right side of change”—one company at a time—remains the cornerstone of our strategy.

Rather than chasing short-term speculation, our focus is on identifying the true long-term winners of innovation such as AI, many of which will soon emerge beyond the infrastructure phase.

Allspring Growth Fund (SGRNX) General Fund Information

Ticker: (SGRNX)

Portfolio managers:

Michael Smith, CFA®;Robert Gruendyke, CFA®;and Christopher Warner, CFA®

Subadvisor: Allspring Global Investments, LLC

Category: Large growth

Fund Strategy

  • Right side of change: Seeks to provide clients long-term compounding through robust growth businesses on the “right side of change”
  • Fundamental “edge”: Seeks to capture the market’s mispricing of future growth through all-cap bottom-up fundamental research
  • Balanced risk management: Style-pure growth focus with a balanced approach to portfolio construction and risk management through a quantified sell discipline

Portfolio holdings and characteristics are subject to change and may have changed since the date specified. The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to purchase or sell a particular security.

Allspring Growth Fund Benchmark Descriptions:

The Russell 3000® Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 3000 companies with higher price/book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with lower price/book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with higher price/book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Diversification does not ensure or guarantee better performance and cannot eliminate the risk of investment losses.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition of Terms:

Active share of an account is calculated by subtracting a portfolio's overlap with its performance benchmark from 100%. The portfolio-level overlap compares parent-level weights between positions held in both the portfolio and benchmark, summing the lesser of such weights.

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

Long-term growth forecast is the annual earnings-per-share growth that the company can sustain over the next 3 or 5 years based on Allspring estimate data.

Price/earnings to growth ratio (PEG ratio) uses long-term earnings growth estimates. The long-term earnings growth is the annual earnings-per-share growth that the company can sustain over the next 3 or 5 years based on estimate data.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled large growth funds, the Growth Fund received 3 stars among 1,004 funds, 2 stars among 936 funds, and 2 stars among 755 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. The Morningstar Rating is for the Institutional Class only; other classes may have different performance characteristics.

The Morningstar absolute ranking is based on the fund's total return rank relative to all funds that have the same category for the same time period. Morningstar rankings do not include the effect of sales charges. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

data.title: Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Equity markets in the developing world, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, rose 4.7% in the fourth quarter, as risk appetites remained largely resilient amid investor enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) and despite tariff risks and geopolitical risks.

Latin America was the best-performing region, climbing 8.2%. Stock markets in Chile and Peru gained in sympathy with the rise in commodity prices. Brazil's stock market rose, thanks to interest rate cuts locally and in the US. Argentina's stock market jumped on news that libertarian President Javier Milei won a landslide victory in midterm elections, handing him a mandate to continue pursuing his radical overhaul of the country's economy.

The region encompassing emerging Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) was the worst-performing region, rising 4.0%. Stock markets in Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic rose, as investors began to anticipate better economic prospects and a possibility of a peace agreement between Russia and Ukraine. South Africa's stock market rose in sympathy with the rise in the price of gold.

Emerging Asia also underperformed, advancing 4.5%. Stock markets in global technology hubs Korea and Taiwan rose on optimism about the demand outlook for chips, the flagship industry of both countries, despite wavering sentiment in November. China's stock market fell on concerns about the country's economic outlook.

Information technology, materials, and energy were the best-performing sectors in the period. Consumer discretionary, health care and communication services were the worst-performing sectors.

(Index returns were measured net of taxes and in US dollar terms.)

Portfolio Review

In the fourth quarter, the Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio rose in absolute terms and outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. (Portfolio returns were measured net of fees and in US dollar terms.)

Helped

  • Stock selection in the consumer discretionary and financials sectors boosted relative performance, as did stock selection in China and Taiwan.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposures to the financials sector and to South Africa and Hungary contributed.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposures to the consumer discretionary and communication services and to China and Saudi Arabia were beneficial to relative performance.
  • Shares of SK Hynix (HXSCF) (3.9% Portfolio weighting) traded higher after the Korea-based chipmaker reported record third-quarter earnings, anchored by high AI-related demand for its chips.
  • Shares of ASE Technology (ASX) (2.7% Portfolio weighting) rose after the Taiwan-based chip packaging and testing provider reported better-than-expected third-quarter results, anchored by robust AI-fueled demand for its services. Shares received an additional boost after the company issued an upbeat outlook due to the AI-related demand.
  • Traders bid up shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) (4.6% Portfolio weighting) after the Taiwan-based contract chipmaker reported better-than-expected third-quarter results. Shares received an additional boost after the company issued a positive revision to its 2025 sales growth forecast to the mid-thirties.
  • Shares of Wiwynn Corp (WYWNF) (1.8% Portfolio weighting) climbed after the Taiwan-based cloud IT infrastructure provider reported better-than-expected quarterly results, highlighted by robust revenue growth and margin expansion, driven by strong demand from hyperscale data-center clients and AI-related server investments.
  • Shares of Yageo (YGPBY) (0.8% Portfolio weighting) advanced after the Taiwan-based chip resistors maker issued an upbeat guidance, with solid domestic demand across all segments and good restocking demand from global distributors.

Hurt

  • Stock selection in the energy sector undercut relative performance, as did stock selection in Brazil and Korea.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposures to the consumer staples sector and Greece detracted.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposures to the information technology and materials sectors and to Taiwan and Korea were detrimental to relative performance.
  • Shares of e-commerce giant Alibaba Group (BABA) (1.1% Portfolio weighting) fell on profit-taking after recent strong stock price appreciation.
  • Shares of Lenovo (LNVGY) (0.9% Portfolio weighting) fell on concerns about the China-based personal computer and data center equipment maker's earning outlook due to concerns that the rising costs of memory would hurt margins and demand in 2026.
  • Taiwan-based contract electronics manufacturer Quanta Computer (QUCCF) (1.6% Portfolio weighting) saw its stock price fall on concerns about margin pressure, which offset the normalization in server and notebook shipments and ongoing exposure to AI-related hardware demand.
  • Shares of GlobalWafers (GWALF) (0.9% Portfolio weighting) receded on concerns about the Taiwan-based silicon wafer maker's earnings outlook due to weaker near-term demand for wafers and delayed customer orders.
  • The stock price of Novatek Microelectronics (NOVKY) (0.7% Portfolio weighting) fell after the Taiwan-based display-driver integrated circuits supplier reported a drop in net profits.

Outlook

Most market fundamentals remain fluid based upon significant geopolitical and trade risks as well as valuations. Our expectations will vary based upon these issues, but, at this juncture, we remain reasonably constructive on emerging markets as long as global growth remains balanced.

Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio (LZEMX)

Important Information

Please consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses carefully before investing. For more complete information about The Lazard Funds, Inc. and current performance, you may obtain a prospectus or summary prospectus by calling 800-823-6300 or going to Lazard Asset Management . Read the prospectus or summary prospectus carefully before you invest. The prospectus and summary prospectus contain investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information about the Portfolio and The Lazard Funds that may not be detailed in this document. The Lazard Funds are distributed by Lazard Asset Management Securities LLC.

The performance quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. An investor may obtain performance data current to the most recent month-end online at Lazard Asset Management . The investment return and principal value of the Portfolio will fluctuate; an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.

Different share classes may have different returns and different investment minimums.

Please click here for standardized returns: Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio

Information and opinions presented have been obtained or derived from sources believed by Lazard Asset Management LLC or its affiliates ("Lazard") to be reliable. Lazard makes no representation as to their accuracy or completeness. All opinions expressed herein are as of the published date and are subject to change.

Allocations and security selection are subject to change.

Mention of these securities should not be considered a recommendation or solicitation to purchase or sell the securities. It should not be assumed that any investment in these securities was, or will prove to be, profitable, or that the investment decisions we make in the future will be profitable or equal to the investment performance of securities referenced herein. There is no assurance that any securities referenced herein are currently held in the portfolio or that securities sold have not been repurchased. The securities mentioned may not represent the entire portfolio.

Equity securities will fluctuate in price; the value of your investment will thus fluctuate, and this may result in a loss. Securities in certain non-domestic countries may be less liquid, more volatile, and less subject to governmental supervision than in one's home market. The values of these securities may be affected by changes in currency rates, application of a country's specific tax laws, changes in government administration, and economic and monetary policy.

Emerging markets securities carry special risks, such as less developed or less efficient trading markets, a lack of company information, and differing auditing and legal standards. The securities markets of emerging markets countries can be extremely volatile; performance can also be influenced by political, social, and economic factors affecting companies in these countries.

The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a free-float-adjusted market capitalization index that is designed to measure equity market performance in the global emerging markets. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index consists of emerging markets country indices including: Brazil, Chile, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Egypt, Greece, Hungary, India, Indonesia, Korea, Malaysia, Mexico, Peru, Philippines, Poland, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey, and United Arab Emirates. The index is unmanaged and has no fees. One cannot invest directly in an index.

data.title: Vornado Realty: Reviewing The 7%+ Preferreds After The Latest Earnings
data.description:
In the following lines, we will take a look at a company that we have reviewed before - Vornado Realty Trust (VNO) and its financial instruments (you can see our last article here). VNO announced its fourth quarter, and we want to find out if there is a difference in financial metrics and instruments. So, let's get started...

VNO in Details

Vornado Realty Trust's current market capitalization is around $6.57 billion. For the 4th quarter of 2025, the company has close to $15.5 billion in total assets, and the total debt is ~ $7.94 billion. The preferred equity of VNO is approximately $1.18 billion. EBITDA is close to $788 million. The portfolio is composed of:

- Оffice properties: 20 million square feet

- Retail properties: 2.4 million square feet

- Marketplaces: 1.6 million square feet

- Residential properties: over 1600 rental apartments

- Studios and other mixed-use facilities

Financial Metrics

Using the information from the earnings, we will calculate some important financial metrics for the company:

  • Asset Yield ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets): 5.08%.
  • Asset Coverage Ratio (total assets/debt): 196%.
  • Operating Expenses/Total Revenue (excluding depreciation and amortization): 57%.
  • Interest Expenses/Debt: 4.16%.
  • Rental Revenue: 10%

We also calculate market-adjusted asset yield to be more accurate. Therefore, we will need a price-to-book value, which is, at the time of writing, 1.29. We replace the book value of equity with its market value by applying the current Price/Book. From here, we can calculate the market-adjusted yield and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio.

  • Market Adjusted Asset Yield: 4.5%.
  • Market Adjusted Asset Coverage ratio: 220.4%.
  • Market Adjusted Rental Revenue (lease revenue/market-adjusted total assets) gross on leasing. : 8.9%
  • Expected AFFO Yield: 5.05%. VNO is among the highest forward P/AFFOs in the sector.

Credibility

The credit rating of the company hasn't changed from its last quarter. Moody's rates it with a "Ba1" credit rating, Fitch rates it with "BB+" and S&P rates it with an "BBB-" credit rating:

We will try to check VNO's credibility, using Moody's approach as always:

- The gross assets of VNO are close to $19.7 billion, and this is equivalent to an "A1" credit rating.

- The unencumbered assets are around 94%, or equivalent to an "A1" credit rating again.

- The secured debt is close to 25% of the gross assets, or this is equivalent to a "Ba2" credit rating.

- The total debt + preferred stock is approximately 46% of the gross assets, or this is a "Baa3" credit rating.

- Net debt to EBITDA: We use the total debt here, and with an EBITDA of $788 million, the ratio is close to 11.6, or a "Ca3" credit rating.

- Fixed charge coverage: EBITDA covers interest and preferred stock dividends at a rate of close to 2 times - "Ba2".

- Market positioning, asset quality, and operating environment: Baa1

The company's operations are largely focused on commercial and office properties.

- Liquidity and access to capital - The operating expenses represent about 57% of revenue, and EBITDA covers interest and preferred share dividends 2 times. Here, the credit rating is equivalent to a "B3."

After calculating all ratios based on market-adjusted characteristics, we obtain a Ba1 credit rating. For the preferreds, we have to go two notches lower and give them a B3.

Preferred Stocks

VNO has four preferred stocks (VNO.PR.L), (VNO.PR.M), (VNO.PR.N), and (VNO.PR.O). All four trade below par currently and have yields to worst of around 7.34-7.47%.

We will look at and use for comparison the highest-yielding VNO.PR.L. The preferred stock has a close to 7.47% current yield. It was issued on 01/16/2013 with a coupon rate of 5.4% and spread to the treasury yield of around 2.65%, while currently this spread is around 2.77%. The call date of the stock has passed, so it is callable anytime. Below you can see its potential current yields vs. prices:

And in the next picture is shown where VNO's stocks are staying among its peers:

Below is shown the REIT OTC bond sector:

The highest yield in the group is around 6.78% and a slightly higher credit rating, so we'll add a 0.2% premium, or 7% yield to maturity. This makes the YTM nearly 0.5% lower than VNO.PR.O's yield. If VNO.PR.O were trading at a yield equivalent to this bond, its price would be around $19.25, representing roughly 6% upside potential from the current price.

OTC Bonds

Above are shown VNO's over-the-counter bonds. The higher yield to maturity is close to 5.4%, with a spread to the treasury yield of around 1.3%. The bonds are rated with a "Ba1" credit rating by Moody's.

Conclusion

VNO once again presents itself with remarkably stable financial and credit indicators. The preferred stocks offer current yields above the sector's average, while credit risk remains moderate.

data.title: Suzano: Expecting Resumed Deleveraging In 2026
data.description:
Suzano S.A. (SUZ) reported the other day, and markets liked it. There was sequential improvement in the markets in terms of pricing on top of considerably more volume thanks to Cerrado and also lower average pulp costs, also thanks to the lower-cost Cerrado footprint contributing to the mix. Improvements in the market, otherwise a little bit on the down, also seem to be materialising as we were picking up on in our last coverage. The next year we expect around 14% FCFY just based on FY 2025 figures, which we believe will be easily on higher volumes and the slight improvement in prices towards the end of the year. Prices have been improving since then as well, and there are a few additional tailwinds that support this price increase into 2026 for pulp.

Results

Some of the salient points in terms of the business' development are that there are revocations of licenses in Indonesia for plantations to produce timbre eventually usable as wood chips and fibre for pulp production. Annual production is around 50 million tonnes globally and the amount affected is already 150k based on curtailments by one producer and could amount to more. With a commodity like this, even a couple hundred bps in change of supply matters. Price increases in pulp are being pushed; the $538 per tonne in the slides is already a backward looking figure. Pulp demand was up in China. The Indonesian revocations are concentrating demand on Vietnamese wood chips, which are raising those prices and affecting costs for Chinese and Japanese manufacturers and others in Asia. Cerrado is one of the most cost effective assets in the world, so higher cash costs for competitors are a good thing and are allowing price increases to be pushed onto customers. The pulp demand recovery in China is also a decent economic indicator, and paper and board production was up 17% in China, meaning end markets aren't getting worse.

For paper and packaging, price increases are happening in export markets as well.

Onto the financials: the Cerrado project is finally wrapped up in terms of CAPEX. CAPEX overall is expected to fall 3 billion BRL into the next year as Cerrado works get lapped.

Meanwhile, operating cash generation, which is net of sustaining CAPEX, was 13 billion BRL in 2025. In 2026, extraordinary CAPEX is supposed to be around 3.6 billion BRL, meaning around 10 billion BRL in FCF thanks to the end of Cerrado's works as well. Cerrado in the mix is also reducing pulp cash costs by a little less than 5% as it's lower cost than existing assets as it comes into force.

Bottom Line

10 billion BRL on around 70 billion BRL in market cap is a considerable FCFY of around 14%. That's a great yield as prices begin to move in the right direction. Deleveraging is the main capital allocation priority. With something up to 10 billion BRL going into net debt reduction with around 65 billion BRL in net debt, deleveraging is around 14% per year. We think the lower risk of financial distress for a commodity company will also increase appraisal of the assets too, so equity is positioned to gain here far ahead of that. It's a great proposition, and Suzano has some of the most competitive assets in the world. Remains a fixture of our portfolio on the deleveraging and eventual dividend angle, which we expect to be quite high on 14% FCF currently, even on limited payout ratios. With the markets bottoming out in pulp, we think that assuming last year's cash generation forward is conservative enough.

data.title: Affirm: Inflection Point From 'Story Stock' To GAAP Profit Machine Is Here
data.description:
Affirm Holdings, Inc. (AFRM) has seen great volatility as of late. This is despite the company posting strong results, with the most important being sustained and increasing GAAP profitability. The company’s incremental operating margins are impressive and bolster my view of the long-term profitability outlook. The stock valuation appears more de-risked than in years past, especially considering the refreshed financial picture. I am upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy" rating.

AFRM Stock Price

I last covered AFRM in November, where I upgraded the stock to a "Buy" because I viewed it as a new leader. The stock has cratered 25% since.

That volatility has de-risked the valuation premium, offering an attractive opportunity for an upgrade.

AFRM Stock Key Metrics

AFRM is a leader in the buy now, pay later industry. Besides being a well-known and popular name with consumers, the company has historically also resonated with investors due to management’s beat-and-raise style. In the most recent quarter, the company delivered gross merchandise volume ("GMV") of $13.8 billion, crushing guidance of between $13 billion and $13.3 billion.

The company has seen sustained hyper-growth in its direct-to-consumer business, led by its Affirm card. I view the Affirm card as being crucial in helping to earn customer loyalty, as it helps differentiate itself from the numerous other BNPL operators.

AFRM has seen its strongest growth in 0% APR transactions. This is an important detail that many bears might be overlooking. I have heard (and at times, made) the argument that BNPL is simply a bad financing option for lower credit scores. I think this misses the whole point. Through 0% offers, AFRM enables merchants to offer promotions without discounting on price. This, in theory, helps retailers avoid the trap of continuous discounting. One could view AFRM then as being similar to online advertising in that it merely earns a sort of “commission” on sales (but I could understand if the reader thinks that I have taken this too far).

I was surprised to see AFRM show 42% YoY growth in active merchants, accelerating considerably even accounting for seasonality. It is important for these BNPL operators to rapidly grow their presence.

AFRM generated 30% YoY revenue growth to $1.123 billion, crushing guidance of between $1.03 billion and $1.06 billion.

Revenue less transaction costs grew at a comparable 29% YoY rate. RLTC and revenues do not always grow in tandem, but the company has seen some stabilization in provisions.

The company generated its third consecutive quarter of positive GAAP operating income. I would have expected the consistent profitability to help it reduce volatility in valuations, but therein lies the opportunity.

I should note, however, that delinquencies have trended higher than each of the prior years. Investors should keep a close eye on this, as delinquencies have been rising in recent years.

The company ended the quarter with $1.1 billion in net cash, which, with the GAAP profitability, represents a stellar balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management noted that the upcoming quarters will be suffering from tough comparables as an enterprise merchant has brought their BNPL solutions in-house.

Nonetheless, management has still guided the company to see up to 27.7% YoY and 24.4% YoY revenue growth in the third and fourth quarters, respectively.

On the conference call, management cited strength in the asset-backed securities market, noting that they were able to price a deal with “a spread of under 100 basis points,” which is something that they hadn’t done “since 2021.” This is an important note, as the company was a pandemic darling and interest rates were lower in 2021. The strong ABS showing indicates that the credit markets have validated AFRM’s business model. Management continues to target the 0% market, noting that it is successful mainly “because it's so easy to understand.” Management also noted their strategic priority of being a “default” payment option with retailers, meaning that their logo would automatically come up at checkout. I view AFRM as being similar to the early days of PayPal (PYPL), one in which there is great value in being a first-mover and taking as much market share as possible, with consumers likely to choose the BNPL option that they have already used before.

Is AFRM Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At recent prices, AFRM traded at just 4x forward sales. That’s a far cry from the 7x sales multiple that it had previously traded at in September. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain robust growth over the coming years.

The company has rapid improvements in profit margins, which forms the basis of my bullish thesis at the moment. As previously seen, the company generated a solid 10% GAAP operating margin and 30% non-GAAP operating margin in the quarter. That improvement comes on the backs of having generated an astounding 47% incremental GAAP operating margin in the quarter.

Perhaps one could quibble about whether the reduction in S&M is sustainable every year, but I would expect the company to realize operational efficiencies in other cost segments as well. But even if we exclude the gains in S&M, the company still generated a 33% incremental GAAP operating margin. I have previously assumed that the company will hit 20% net margins over the long term. Between the company’s third consecutive quarter of GAAP profitability (including again a 10% GAAP operating margin) and the high incremental margins, I am growing of the view that 20% might prove too conservative. Let’s stick with 20%. That places the stock at just 20x long-term earnings. Against 24% forward growth, that is a less than 1x price-to-earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’). I could see AFRM re-rating higher, but the stock looks priced for at least 15% to 20% annual returns even if one assumes some multiple compression along the way.

So why is the stock trading at more reasonable valuations? I do not think it is due to the proposed 10% cap on credit card rates, as I would expect any reduced demand for credit cards to be a tailwind for BNPL operators like AFRM. I instead think the reason is due to concerns that the economy may be slowing down. Perhaps investors are also afraid that AI might lead to widespread layoffs, which might hurt consumer spending. My crystal ball is not telling me anything, and AFRM is likely to see volatility in this scenario. It is highly possible that consensus estimates prove too optimistic in the near term. That said, I view BNPL as more than just a trend and something that is absolutely here to stay, especially due to the aforementioned marketing benefits. The company’s GAAP profitability and net cash balance sheet may help insulate it from any future recessions. I can see the stock working out even if long-term growth comes in at just 15% on average over the next few years (though I find that unlikely). I am upgrading the stock to a strong buy, but note that the market is likely to still view this as being highly sensitive to a weak economy, meaning that volatility cannot be avoided.

AFRM Stock Risks

The economy might slip into a recession. AFRM might see elevated delinquencies alongside lower GMV. It is possible that AI disrupts the industry, perhaps allowing an upstart to offer a cheaper product. AFRM is not yet that profitable on a GAAP basis, and thus the stock is likely to sustain great volatility. More big retailers might bring BNPL operations in-house, perhaps helped by vibe coding.

AFRM Stock Conclusion

The market continues to view AFRM as being a high-risk, high-reward name. The market is not giving the company enough credit for the sustained top-line growth as well as the rapidly improving GAAP profit margins. Growth rates might face volatility in a recession, but I expect AFRM to remain a leader in the resilient BNPL industry. AFRM is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: Amazon's Biggest Investments Ever Could Lead To Its Quietest Stock Year
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) guided ~$200b in 2026 capex, with a majority of the spending going to AWS for AI - one of the largest single-year investments in corporate history. This thesis looks at the impact of this spend on the stock and finds the initial correction in share prices to be in line with what has been historically observed through large buildout phases - that is, a period of capped upside for the next few quarters.

Capex spending has not always been punished across the tech leadership in the current AI race, but Amazon's case faces pressure due to cash conversion cycle delays and the extent of FCF impact. That should keep Amazon on Hold, despite Q4 numbers otherwise showing encouraging trends. The long-term Buy thesis remains intact, and Amazon will bounce back strongly once utilization ramps up, but it may go through a prolonged consolidation in the near term. I retain my Hold rating, expecting better time-adjusted and absolute entry points in the future.

FCF Impact A Concern

Amazon has been through a build phase in the past around 2016-17, 2021-22, and now this phase that stretches from 2024. These periods of rising capex have meant pressure on the FCF. The stock (and valuation) has often stayed under pressure too, doing most of the running in the harvesting periods between these cycles.

The magnitude of capex involved in the current cycle is far higher. Capex has been surging from ~$50b in 2023 to ~$80b in 2024 and ~$125b for 2025 (collated through earnings call transcripts data and guidance). And the relentless uptick continues, even bigger, in 2026. These are unprecedented levels of capex and potentially the most prolonged - of course, in response to a once-in-a-generation AI opportunity.

Through this capex cycle, Amazon has been going through an expected FCF decline, with the most recent TTM FCF reported as ~$11.2b. The operating cash flow reported in 2025 is ~$139b. Most of the conversion from operating cash flow to free cash flow is absorbed by ~$125b in capex (lower impact of working capital). With around $75b increase expected in 2026, and assuming similar FCF conversion dynamics and ~20% operating cash flow growth, similar to 2025 (projected hence to ~$166b), Amazon is likely moving toward a negative FCF regime again (anywhere between -$30b and -$50b).

This is where the near-term story comes under pressure - the extent of FCF decline and a potential drag on AWS operating margins too. Depreciation on data center assets could span years, so operating income absorbs non-cash expenses until utilization peaks. Management also cautioned against straight-line expectations around operating margins through a heavy build phase.

Naturally, the markets reacted negatively to the capex news because it means the free cash flow gets pulled forward materially. Near-term margins will compress, and there will be delayed payoff at a time when markets are more uncomfortable with underwriting long-duration growth.

Why Capex Matters For Amazon

Heavy capital spending by itself is not the primary determinant of share prices. It hasn't been in this AI race cycle of the past couple of years. What matters is how quickly the spend translates into visible revenue and earnings. Markets have differentiated capex spending on this cash conversion timing variable across various stocks. And the capex size is just one variable there.

Google recently announced a comparable and substantial capex plan for 2026 - ~$180b at the midpoint. This is certainly still a drag, but the markets appear to be more supportive so far, even after a strong rally in the last 6 months, whereas Amazon has been consolidating before and still corrected significantly. This is potentially because Google's core search and YouTube advertising businesses generate substantial operating cash flow even as AI and data center investments rise, keeping free cash flow relatively healthy.

On the other hand, markets have not entirely overlooked the capex drag on Microsoft and Meta Platforms. Both companies have seen periods of investor caution and increased focus on growth and margins, keeping the share prices in check. Tesla is a different story, where the narrative is strengthened and FCF is traditionally a less important predictor of share prices, but even there I feel share prices are supported due to the current substantial build phase at best; upside is again pressured.

Overall, capex matters more for Amazon at this point because it has already been under a lot of free cash flow pressure in the recent past, and the absolute damage in cash flow is potentially the most heightened. The difference is that Amazon is entering this next leg of the investment cycle from a much weaker free cash flow position. This makes the near-term financial optics more strained and delays the point at which markets begin to reward the long-term earnings potential being built today. The fact that the investments are in the longer cash conversion end of this spectrum also makes it a bigger factor for Amazon. Most of the spend is expected to go toward building data centers and compute infrastructure - revenue and cash flow come in much later after utilization ramps.

Amazon also reacts differently because of its structure. Amazon’s business mix includes capital-intensive logistics operations too, along with the cloud services, which are also infrastructure-heavy. Both require ongoing reinvestment and generate higher depreciation compared to an advertising-driven business model. As a result, margins and free cash flow tend to be more volatile during build phases.

This Time Is Different

The last time FCF turned significantly negative was in 2022. This also coincided with a widespread equity market downturn and, crucially, was a period of overbuilding alongside slowing demand (after the post-pandemic highs). So, while Amazon corrected significantly by ~50% in that negative FCF regime, this time is quite different. Demand is accelerating, and AI monetization prospects reduce the chances of overbuilding. That is why I expect the share price reaction to be muted versus 2022, mostly balancing the drag from the capex cycle.

The long-term thesis also appears less uncertain. Amazon is broadly betting on AI workload and cloud workload growth to structurally rise for the next decade from here, and that secular push is fundamentally supported. Demand concerns are not the gating variable in valuing Amazon right now. Signals are structurally strong, as reported in Q4 2025. Growth was described as supply constrained. 3.9 GWs were added in the past 12 months. The AWS backlog stands at $244b, up ~40% YoY. What Amazon is building should re-accelerate AWS revenue as AI workloads keep scaling, but payoffs are multi-year and back-end loaded.

This time around, core operating trends also remain solid. AWS revenue grew 24% YoY in Q4 to a $142b annualized run rate, the fastest growth in 13 quarters. Advertising also posted solid growth of ~22% YoY. North America retail operating margin improved to 9% vs. 8% YoY despite continued fulfillment investment. The revenue trajectory is expected to remain robust in 2026. As more capacity comes online, AWS could keep growing at current average run rates of ~24%, adding $30-35 billion in revenue annually. Even assuming operating margins mature toward 30% (from ~35% currently), that's about $9b added as incremental operating income per year as the build cycle matures.

So overall, the long-term thesis stays intact for patient capital. The Amazon story is definitely not looking broken, although it may remain capped as the markets acknowledge the long-dated risks and digest the more visible near-term earnings and cash flow impacts. The risks to the long-duration benefits here are lower-than-expected long-term AI demand and lower margins later due to pricing pressure from competition. For the immediate future, though, I expect Amazon to stay range-bound and offer better entry points as the build cycle matures and after a period of time correction. When Amazon starts rerating after such a phase, it is usually a substantial move - so the costs of waiting for a better entry point (in terms of missing out entirely) do not outweigh the benefits of near-term consolidation and risk of corrections, in my view.

data.title: Performance Insights - Monthly Report: February 2026
data.description:
Equities look past geopolitical risks; market leadership remains outside the US.

Oil rose in January on multiple supply shocks; gold and silver saw a correction.

However, the growth and inflation picture remained supportive of risk assets.

Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
